title: Second Letter 'e'
desc: Find products whose second letter is 'e' using LIKE with the _ wildcard. Show all columns.
data:
INSERT INTO products VALUES
  (1,'Wireless Mouse','Electronics',29.99,150),
  (2,'Mechanical Keyboard','Electronics',89.99,75),
  (3,'USB-C Hub','Electronics',45.00,200),
  (4,'Desk Lamp','Furniture',35.50,60),
  (5,'Ergonomic Chair','Furniture',299.99,25),
  (6,'Notebook Set','Stationery',12.99,500),
  (7,'Whiteboard Markers','Stationery',8.99,300),
  (8,'Monitor Stand','Furniture',55.00,90),
  (9,'Webcam HD','Electronics',79.99,45),
  (10,'Stapler','Stationery',6.50,180);
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE '_e%'
hint: _ matches exactly one character. LIKE '_e%' means: any first character, then 'e', then anything. Products like 'Desk Lamp' (D-e) and 'Mechanical Keyboard' (M-e) match.

title: Safe Budget
desc: Show each task description and its effective hours budget, treating missing budgets as 0.
schema:
CREATE TABLE assigned_tasks (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, description TEXT NOT NULL,
  assignee_id INTEGER, hours_budget REAL,
  completed INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0);
data:

  (3,'Write tests',NULL,NULL,0),
  (4,'Refactor auth',6,12.0,0),
  (5,'Design review',2,NULL,1),
  (6,'Deploy v2',NULL,16.0,0),
  (7,'Performance audit',9,6.0,0),
  (8,'Code review',NULL,NULL,1),
  (9,'Server migration',6,20.0,0),
  (10,'Documentation',5,NULL,1);
solution: SELECT description, COALESCE(hours_budget, 0) AS effective_budget FROM assigned_tasks
hint: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL value. COALESCE(hours_budget, 0) replaces NULL with 0.

CREATE TABLE assigned_tasks (

title: NULLs Sort First
desc: List all tasks sorted by hours_budget (NULLs first). When multiple tasks have the same hours_budget, break ties alphabetically by description.
schema:
CREATE TABLE assigned_tasks (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, description TEXT NOT NULL,
  assignee_id INTEGER, hours_budget REAL,
  completed INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0);
data:
INSERT INTO assigned_tasks VALUES

  (2,'Fix API bug',1,4.0,1),
  (3,'Write tests',NULL,NULL,0),
  (4,'Refactor auth',6,12.0,0),

title: The Wrong Way

schema:
CREATE TABLE assigned_tasks (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, description TEXT NOT NULL,
  assignee_id INTEGER, hours_budget REAL,
  completed INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0);
data:
INSERT INTO assigned_tasks VALUES

  (7,'Performance audit',9,6.0,0),
  (8,'Code review',NULL,NULL,1),
  (9,'Server migration',6,20.0,0),
  (10,'Documentation',5,NULL,1);

hint: NULL is not a value — it means 'unknown'. Comparisons like = NULL always return false/unknown. Always use IS NULL.

  (1,'Alice','Engineering','New York'),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing','Chicago'),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering','New York'),
  (4,'Diana','HR','Boston'),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing','Chicago'),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering','San Francisco'),
  (7,'Grace','HR','Boston'),
  (8,'Ivy','Sales','Chicago');
INSERT INTO alumni VALUES
  (1,'Charlie','Engineering','New York'),
  (2,'Eve','Marketing','Chicago'),
  (3,'Karen','Engineering','San Francisco'),
  (4,'Leo','Sales','New York'),
  (5,'Nina','HR','Boston'),
  (6,'Omar','Support','New York');
solution: SELECT name FROM current_employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' UNION SELECT name FROM alumni WHERE department = 'Engineering' ORDER BY name
hint: Each SELECT in a UNION can have its own WHERE filter. ORDER BY applies to the whole result.

  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL,
  city TEXT NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
hint: Same as the previous problem but with LEFT JOIN to keep employees who have no manager (NULL manager_id).

title: Same-Department Colleagues
desc: Show pairs of employees who work in the same department. Each pair should appear once.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL,
  city TEXT NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT a.name AS employee, b.name AS colleague, a.department FROM employees a INNER JOIN employees b ON a.department = b.department AND a.id < b.id
hint: Self-join with a.id < b.id to pair each employee with colleagues alphabetically and avoid pairing someone with themselves.

title: Three-Level Chain
desc: Show each employee, their manager, and their manager's manager (senior manager).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL,
  city TEXT NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager, mm.name AS senior_manager FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id LEFT JOIN employees mm ON m.manager_id = mm.id
hint: Chain LEFT JOINs: first join to get the manager, second join to get the manager's manager. Each level can be NULL.

title: Avg Salary by Department Location
desc: Calculate the average salary of employees at each department location, rounded to 2 decimal places.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL,
  city TEXT NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE departments (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL,
  location TEXT NOT NULL, budget INTEGER NOT NULL);
data:

  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
INSERT INTO departments VALUES
  (1,'Engineering','Building A',500000),
  (2,'Marketing','Building B',300000),
  (3,'HR','Building A',200000),
  (4,'Sales','Building C',250000),

solution: SELECT d.location, ROUND(AVG(e.salary), 2) AS avg_salary FROM departments d INNER JOIN employees e ON d.name = e.department GROUP BY d.location
hint: JOIN departments to employees, GROUP BY location, then compute ROUND(AVG(salary), 2).

title: Budget Per Employee Department
desc: Show each department's budget divided by its employee count (budget per employee), rounded to 2 decimal places.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL,
  city TEXT NOT NULL, manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE departments (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL,
  location TEXT NOT NULL, budget INTEGER NOT NULL);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
INSERT INTO departments VALUES
  (1,'Engineering','Building A',500000),
  (2,'Marketing','Building B',300000),
  (3,'HR','Building A',200000),
  (4,'Sales','Building C',250000),
  (5,'Support','Building B',150000);
solution: SELECT d.name AS department, d.budget, COUNT(e.id) AS employees, ROUND(CAST(d.budget AS REAL) / COUNT(e.id), 2) AS budget_per_employee FROM departments d LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.name = e.department GROUP BY d.id
hint: Division in SQL produces a float. CAST to REAL first, then ROUND to 2 decimals. LEFT JOIN includes departments with 0 employees.

title: Has Orders (EXISTS)

solution: SELECT * FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE employee_id = e.id)
hint: EXISTS returns TRUE if the subquery returns any rows. SELECT 1 is a convention.

title: Department Salary Summary
desc: Using a derived table, find departments where max-min salary exceeds $8,000.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:

  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),

  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),

  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL,
  order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
solution: SELECT name, total FROM (SELECT e.name, SUM(o.amount) AS total FROM employees e JOIN orders o ON e.id = o.employee_id GROUP BY e.id ORDER BY total DESC, e.name) LIMIT 3
hint: ORDER BY inside a derived table only matters with LIMIT. The name tiebreaker makes ties deterministic.

title: Big Spenders via Derived Table
desc: Using a derived table in an IN subquery, find employees with total orders over $1,000.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL,
  order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
solution: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id IN (SELECT employee_id FROM (SELECT employee_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY employee_id) WHERE total > 1000)
hint: A derived table can be used inside an IN subquery.

title: Nested Count
desc: Using a derived table, find the department with the most employees.
schema:

  manager_id INTEGER);

title: The Concat NULL Trap
desc: Build 'name's manager_id is manager_id' as mgr_info using concatenation. Some employees have NULL manager_id — observe what happens to their row. (Trap: || with NULL produces NULL for the whole string.)
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name || '''s manager_id is ' || manager_id AS mgr_info FROM employees
hint: In SQLite, 'abc' || NULL evaluates to NULL, not 'abc'. The entire concatenated string disappears when any part is NULL — this is the || NULL trap.

title: NULL Trap Fix (COALESCE)
desc: Build 'name's manager_id is manager_id' as mgr_info, but fix the NULL problem using COALESCE to show 'None' instead of NULL for employees without a manager.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name || '''s manager_id is ' || COALESCE(CAST(manager_id AS TEXT), 'None') AS mgr_info FROM employees
hint: COALESCE replaces NULL with a default value. CAST(manager_id AS TEXT) is needed because || expects strings, but COALESCE works on any compatible type.

title: Contains 'er'
desc: Find all products whose name contains the substring 'er' anywhere (case-insensitive).
data:
INSERT INTO products VALUES
  (1,'Wireless Mouse','Electronics',29.99,150),
  (2,'Mechanical Keyboard','Electronics',89.99,75),
  (3,'USB-C Hub','Electronics',45.00,200),
  (4,'Desk Lamp','Furniture',35.50,60),
  (5,'Ergonomic Chair','Furniture',299.99,25),
  (6,'Notebook Set','Stationery',12.99,500),
  (7,'Whiteboard Markers','Stationery',8.99,300),
  (8,'Monitor Stand','Furniture',55.00,90),
  (9,'Webcam HD','Electronics',79.99,45),
  (10,'Stapler','Stationery',6.50,180);
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%er%'
hint: LIKE '%er%' matches strings with 'er' anywhere — at the start, middle, or end. The % on both sides means 'anything before and after'.

title: Products Starting with W (GLOB)
desc: Find all products whose name starts with 'W' using GLOB. Note: GLOB is case-sensitive, so 'W*' matches uppercase W only.
data:
INSERT INTO products VALUES
  (1,'Wireless Mouse','Electronics',29.99,150),
  (2,'Mechanical Keyboard','Electronics',89.99,75),
  (3,'USB-C Hub','Electronics',45.00,200),
  (4,'Desk Lamp','Furniture',35.50,60),
  (5,'Ergonomic Chair','Furniture',299.99,25),
  (6,'Notebook Set','Stationery',12.99,500),
  (7,'Whiteboard Markers','Stationery',8.99,300),
  (8,'Monitor Stand','Furniture',55.00,90),
  (9,'Webcam HD','Electronics',79.99,45),
  (10,'Stapler','Stationery',6.50,180);
solution: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name GLOB 'W*'
hint: GLOB uses * as wildcard (not %). GLOB 'W*' matches 'W' followed by anything. Unlike LIKE, GLOB cares about case: 'w*' would match nothing here since all products start with uppercase.

title: GLOB Case-Sensitivity Contrast
desc: Show supplier names that start with 'e' using GLOB. Use GLOB 'e*' — case-sensitive lowercase. Then explain why 'echo materials' matches but 'Echo Materials' wouldn't.
data:
INSERT INTO suppliers VALUES
  (1,'  Acme Parts  ','Detroit','Active'),
  (2,'Beta Supply  ','Chicago','active'),
  (3,'  Gamma Goods','New York','Active'),
  (4,'Delta Dist','  Boston  ','inactive'),
  (5,'echo materials','Atlanta','Active'),
  (6,'  Foxtrot Inc  ','Miami','Inactive'),
  (7,'Zulu Co','Seattle','Active');
solution: SELECT * FROM suppliers WHERE name GLOB 'e*'
hint: Unlike LIKE (case-insensitive for ASCII), GLOB matches exact case. GLOB 'e*' only matches lowercase 'e'. GLOB 'E*' matches uppercase 'E'. Only row 5 ('echo materials') starts with lowercase 'e'.

title: Price as Integer

title: Average Name Length (CAST)
desc: Calculate the average character length of employee names and cast it to INTEGER as avg_name_len. (Truncation expected.)
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT CAST(AVG(LENGTH(name)) AS INTEGER) AS avg_name_len FROM employees
hint: AVG(LENGTH(name)) = 4.4. CAST(4.4 AS INTEGER) truncates to 4. Use CAST when you need an integer, not ROUND.

title: Department Email

  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:

  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),

title: Full Supplier Cleanup

desc: Show each employee's name, hire date, and the date 90 days later as plus_90_days.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, hire_date, date(hire_date, '+90 days') AS plus_90_days FROM employees
hint: Using '+N days' shifts the date forward by N calendar days.

title: Days Since Hire
desc: Show each employee's name and the number of days between 2025-01-01 and their hire_date as days_since, rounded to 0 decimals.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, ROUND(julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date), 0) AS days_since FROM employees
hint: julianday(date) returns a fractional day count. Subtract two julianday values to get the difference in days. ROUND(... , 0) removes decimal noise. Earlier hires have larger values.

title: Tenure in Years
desc: Show each employee's name and their tenure in years as of 2025-01-01 as tenure_years, rounded to 1 decimal.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, ROUND((julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25, 1) AS tenure_years FROM employees
hint: Divide day difference by 365.25 to approximate years. ROUND to 1 decimal for a clean report.

title: Days to Deadline
desc: From the projects table, show each project name and its deadline, and the days between 2024-01-01 and the deadline as days_to_deadline, rounded to 0 decimals.
solution: SELECT name AS project, deadline, ROUND(julianday(deadline) - julianday('2024-01-01'), 0) AS days_to_deadline FROM projects
hint: julianday(deadline) - julianday('2024-01-01') counts days from Jan 1. Sort order shows sooner deadlines first.

title: Hire to Deadline Days
desc: Using employees and projects, show each project name, its lead's name, and the days between the lead's hire_date and the project's deadline as days_diff, rounded to 0 decimals. Only include projects with a lead.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE projects (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL,
  lead_id INTEGER, deadline TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
INSERT INTO projects VALUES
  (1,'Alpha',1,'2024-06-01','Active'),
  (2,'Beta',3,'2024-07-15','Active'),
  (3,'Gamma',6,'2024-08-01','Active'),
  (4,'Delta',NULL,'2024-09-01','Planning'),
  (5,'Epsilon',1,'2024-05-15','Completed'),
  (6,'Zeta',NULL,'2024-10-01','Planning'),
  (7,'Eta',9,'2024-06-30','Active');
solution: SELECT p.name AS project, e.name AS lead, ROUND(julianday(p.deadline) - julianday(e.hire_date), 0) AS days_diff FROM projects p INNER JOIN employees e ON p.lead_id = e.id
hint: Subtract one date from another using julianday. INNER JOIN on lead_id ensures only projects with leads.

title: Department Code (Simple CASE)
desc: Show each employee's name, department, and a short code using a simple CASE: Engineering→'ENG', Marketing→'MKT', HR→'HR', Sales→'SAL'
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, department, CASE department WHEN 'Engineering' THEN 'ENG' WHEN 'Marketing' THEN 'MKT' WHEN 'HR' THEN 'HR' WHEN 'Sales' THEN 'SAL' END AS code FROM employees
hint: Simple CASE compares one expression (here 'department') to multiple WHEN values. No ELSE means unmatched departments return NULL.

title: Salary Band (Searched CASE)
desc: Show each employee's name, salary, and a salary band: '< 60k' for salary < 60000, '60k-80k' for 60000-79999, '80k+' for 80000+, as band.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, salary, CASE WHEN salary < 60000 THEN '< 60k' WHEN salary < 80000 THEN '60k-80k' ELSE '80k+' END AS band FROM employees
hint: Searched CASE evaluates boolean conditions in order. The first true branch wins. ELSE catches everything else.

title: Custom Department Sort
desc: Show all employees sorted by a custom department priority using CASE in ORDER BY: Engineering first, Marketing second, HR third, Sales fourth, all others last. Within the same priority, sort by name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, department FROM employees ORDER BY CASE department WHEN 'Engineering' THEN 1 WHEN 'Marketing' THEN 2 WHEN 'HR' THEN 3 WHEN 'Sales' THEN 4 ELSE 5 END, name
hint: CASE in ORDER BY assigns a numeric priority to each row. The result is sorted by that number, then by name. Each department gets one priority value, so no ties within a priority group exist.

title: CASE NULL Trap
desc: Show each employee's name, salary, and a CASE expression: 'High' when salary >= 100000, no ELSE. Watch what happens to employees who don't meet the condition — CASE without ELSE returns NULL, not the original value.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, salary, CASE WHEN salary >= 100000 THEN 'High' END AS label FROM employees
hint: CASE without ELSE returns NULL when no WHEN condition matches. No employee here has salary >= 100000, so ALL rows get NULL in the label column.

title: CASE NULL Fix (ELSE)
desc: Show each employee's name, salary, and salary category: 'High' for >= 100000, 'Standard' for everything else, as category. (Fixes the P715 trap with ELSE.)
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, salary, CASE WHEN salary >= 100000 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Standard' END AS category FROM employees
hint: ELSE provides a default when no WHEN matches. Without it, CASE returns NULL — a common source of bugs.

CREATE TABLE employees (

  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),

title: Hire Quarter
desc: Show each employee's name, hire date, and the quarter they were hired (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) as quarter using strftime and CASE.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, hire_date, CASE CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) WHEN 1 THEN 'Q1' WHEN 2 THEN 'Q1' WHEN 3 THEN 'Q1' WHEN 4 THEN 'Q2' WHEN 5 THEN 'Q2' WHEN 6 THEN 'Q2' WHEN 7 THEN 'Q3' WHEN 8 THEN 'Q3' WHEN 9 THEN 'Q3' WHEN 10 THEN 'Q4' WHEN 11 THEN 'Q4' WHEN 12 THEN 'Q4' END AS quarter FROM employees
hint: Extract the month number with strftime('%m'), CAST to INTEGER, then use a simple CASE to map months 1-3→Q1, 4-6→Q2, 7-9→Q3, 10-12→Q4.

title: Tenure Category
desc: Show each employee's name, hire date, and tenure category as of 2025-01-01: '< 3y' if less than 3 years, '3-5y' if 3 to 5.99 years, '5y+' if 6 or more years. Use julianday for the calculation, rounded to 1 decimal.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, hire_date, CASE WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 3 THEN '< 3y' WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 6 THEN '3-5y' ELSE '5y+' END AS tenure_category FROM employees
hint: Use julianday difference / 365.25 for year approximation, then CASE to bucket into groups. The ELSE catches 6+ years.

title: Pre-2020 Seniority
desc: Show each employee's name, hire date, and a seniority flag: 'Senior' if hired before 2020, 'Junior' if hired in 2020 or later. Use strftime to extract the year.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, hire_date, CASE WHEN strftime('%Y', hire_date) < '2020' THEN 'Senior' ELSE 'Junior' END AS seniority FROM employees
hint: Compare the strftime result as a string — '2019' < '2020' works correctly because of ISO date format's lexical ordering.

title: Project Urgency
desc: Show each project name, deadline, and an urgency label using CASE: 'Overdue' if deadline < '2024-07-01', 'Soon' if deadline < '2024-09-01', 'Later' otherwise.
solution: SELECT name AS project, deadline, CASE WHEN deadline < '2024-07-01' THEN 'Overdue' WHEN deadline < '2024-09-01' THEN 'Soon' ELSE 'Later' END AS urgency FROM projects
hint: SQLite compares ISO date strings lexicographically — '2024-06-01' < '2024-07-01' works directly without conversion.

title: Multi-Field CASE
desc: Show each employee's name, department, salary band ('< 60k', '60k-80k', '80k+'), and tenure category ('< 3y', '3-5y', '5y+') in a single query. Use CASE for both computed columns.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, department, CASE WHEN salary < 60000 THEN '< 60k' WHEN salary < 80000 THEN '60k-80k' ELSE '80k+' END AS band, CASE WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 3 THEN '< 3y' WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 6 THEN '3-5y' ELSE '5y+' END AS tenure_category FROM employees
hint: You can use multiple independent CASE expressions in one SELECT. Each computes its own column.

title: Average Tenure by Dept
desc: Show each department name and the average tenure of its employees in years as avg_tenure, rounded to 1 decimal. Use julianday with reference date 2025-01-01.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),

solution: SELECT department, ROUND(AVG((julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25), 1) AS avg_tenure FROM employees GROUP BY department
hint: Compute tenure in the AVG() function, then ROUND the result. GROUP BY department gives one row per department.

title: Weekday Hire Count
desc: Count how many employees were hired on each weekday (0=Sunday … 6=Saturday). Show weekday number and count as hired_count.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT strftime('%w', hire_date) AS weekday, COUNT(*) AS hired_count FROM employees GROUP BY weekday
hint: strftime('%w') in GROUP BY aggregates by day-of-week. Cast to INTEGER if you want numeric ordering.

title: Tenure and Salary Tier
desc: Show each employee's name and a tier computed by nested logic: if tenure < 3y → 'New', if 3-5y then if salary >= 80000 → 'Experienced Senior' else 'Experienced', if 5y+ then if salary >= 90000 → 'Veteran Lead' else 'Veteran'. Use CASE with AND conditions (searched CASE).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6);
solution: SELECT name, CASE WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 3 THEN 'New' WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 6 AND salary >= 80000 THEN 'Experienced Senior' WHEN (julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25 < 6 THEN 'Experienced' WHEN salary >= 90000 THEN 'Veteran Lead' ELSE 'Veteran' END AS tier FROM employees
hint: Searched CASE with AND lets you combine conditions. Order matters — the first true WHEN wins. Less-than-6 checks must come before the 6+ else.

title: Project Duration Analysis
desc: Show each project name, its lead's name, the lead's hire date, the project deadline, and the months between hire and deadline as months_to_deadline rounded to 1 decimal. Only projects with a lead. Calculate months as (julianday diff) / 30.4375.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE projects (

  lead_id INTEGER, deadline TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL);

  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),

  (2,'Beta',3,'2024-07-15','Active'),

solution: SELECT p.name AS project, e.name AS lead, e.hire_date, p.deadline, ROUND((julianday(p.deadline) - julianday(e.hire_date)) / 30.4375, 1) AS months_to_deadline FROM projects p INNER JOIN employees e ON p.lead_id = e.id
hint: Divide day difference by 30.4375 (average days per month) for approximate months. ROUND to 1 decimal. Projects led by recently hired employees have smaller values.

title: Big Spenders Chain
desc: Chain two CTEs: (1) total order amount per employee, (2) employees who spent more than 1000 total. Show their name, department, and total spent.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL,
  order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
solution: WITH order_totals AS (SELECT employee_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY employee_id), big_spenders AS (SELECT employee_id, total_spent FROM order_totals WHERE total_spent > 1000) SELECT e.name, e.department, b.total_spent FROM employees e INNER JOIN big_spenders b ON e.id = b.employee_id
hint: order_totals aggregates the raw orders, then big_spenders keeps only the high-value results. The main query joins to get employee details.

schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:

  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),

solution: WITH avg_sal AS (SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_val FROM employees) SELECT name, salary, ROUND(salary - avg_val, 0) AS diff FROM employees CROSS JOIN avg_sal WHERE salary > avg_val ORDER BY diff DESC, name

  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:

title: CTE in IN and NOT IN
desc: Create a CTE for project lead IDs (non-NULL). Use the same CTE in two subqueries: IN to list employees with projects, and NOT IN to list those without.

solution: WITH project_leads AS (SELECT DISTINCT lead_id AS id FROM projects WHERE lead_id IS NOT NULL) SELECT e.name, 'Has Project' AS status FROM employees e WHERE e.id IN (SELECT id FROM project_leads) UNION ALL SELECT e.name, 'No Project' FROM employees e WHERE e.id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM project_leads)
hint: The same CTE feeds both IN and NOT IN. Note: NOT IN is safe here because the CTE filters out NULLs (no NULL trap).

title: CTE in SELECT and ORDER BY
desc: Create a CTE computing each department's average salary. Reference it in the SELECT clause (to get avg_salary next to each employee) and in ORDER BY (to sort by department average).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH dept_avg AS (SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department) SELECT e.name, e.department, e.salary, ROUND(d.avg_sal, 0) AS dept_avg_salary FROM employees e INNER JOIN dept_avg d ON e.department = d.department ORDER BY d.avg_sal DESC, e.salary DESC
hint: The CTE is joined for the SELECT columns and also used for ORDER BY. Employees appear sorted by their department's average salary, then by their own salary within the department.

title: Flatten a Nested Subquery
desc: Find employees in departments whose average salary exceeds the company-wide average. Use chained CTEs instead of nested subqueries. Show name, department, salary.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH company_avg AS (SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees), dept_avgs AS (SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department), high_depts AS (SELECT department FROM dept_avgs WHERE avg_sal > (SELECT avg_sal FROM company_avg)) SELECT e.name, e.department, e.salary FROM employees e WHERE e.department IN (SELECT department FROM high_depts)
hint: Without CTEs this would be 3 levels of nesting: WHERE department IN (SELECT department FROM employees GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary) > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)). The CTE version reads top-to-bottom instead of inside-out.

title: Salary Spread by Department

CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

solution: WITH dept_spread AS (SELECT department, MAX(salary) - MIN(salary) AS spread FROM employees GROUP BY department) SELECT e.name, e.department, e.salary, d.spread FROM employees e INNER JOIN dept_spread d ON e.department = d.department ORDER BY d.spread DESC, e.salary DESC
hint: The CTE encapsulates the spread calculation. The main query is simple: join employees to their department's spread and sort. Separating calculation from display is the CTE's readability win.

title: Count to Ten
desc: Use a recursive CTE to generate the numbers 1 through 10. Show the number as n.
solution: WITH RECURSIVE numbers(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n + 1 FROM numbers WHERE n < 10) SELECT n FROM numbers
hint: The base case (SELECT 1) starts the sequence. The recursive step adds 1 each iteration until n reaches 10. The WHERE clause is essential — it terminates the recursion.

title: Org Chart with Level
desc: Use a recursive CTE to walk the employee-manager hierarchy top-down. Show each employee's id, name, and management level (0 for top managers), ordered by level then name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, ot.level + 1 FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id) SELECT id, name, level FROM org_tree ORDER BY level, name
hint: Base case: employees with no manager (the top-level managers). Recursive step: find employees whose manager is the previous level's result. Adds 1 to level each step.

title: Manager Chain (Bottom-Up)
desc: Use a recursive CTE starting from Kate (id=11) to walk up the management chain. Show each employee's id, name, and depth (0 for Kate), ordered by depth descending.

  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),

hint: Bottom-up recursion joins e.id = mc.manager_id — inverse of the top-down pattern. This walks up the chain: Kate → Alice → Frank. ORDER BY depth DESC shows the CEO first.

title: Org Path (Hierarchical Names)
desc: Use a recursive CTE to build the full hierarchical path for each employee. Show id, name, and the path as a string of names separated by ' → '. Start from top managers.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_path AS (SELECT id, name, manager_id, name AS path FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, op.path || ' → ' || e.name FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_path op ON e.manager_id = op.id) SELECT id, name, path FROM org_path ORDER BY id
hint: The path is built incrementally: each recursive step appends the current employee's name to the parent's path using ||. Base case: top managers have just their own name.

title: Reports per Top Manager
desc: Use a recursive CTE to assign each employee to their ultimate top manager. Then count total reports (direct + indirect) per top manager. Show manager name and total_reports, sorted by count descending then manager name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),

  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),

solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_reports AS (SELECT id, manager_id, id AS top_id FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.manager_id, ot.top_id FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_reports ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id) SELECT e.name AS manager, COUNT(*) AS total_reports FROM org_reports o INNER JOIN employees e ON o.top_id = e.id GROUP BY o.top_id ORDER BY total_reports DESC, e.name
hint: The CTE assigns every employee a top_id (their ultimate root manager). The main query counts employees per top_id. This counts the manager themselves — use COUNT(*) - 1 to exclude them.

solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_depth AS (SELECT id, manager_id, 0 AS depth FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.manager_id, od.depth + 1 FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_depth od ON e.manager_id = od.id) SELECT depth, COUNT(*) AS employee_count FROM org_depth GROUP BY depth ORDER BY depth
hint: After computing each employee's depth, GROUP BY depth gives the distribution. This shows how 'tall' the org is and which level has the most employees.

title: Moving Average Salary (3-Row)
desc: Show name, hire_date, salary, and a 3-row moving average rounded to 1 decimal as mov_avg. Frame: current row + 2 preceding rows.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),

hint: ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW creates a sliding 3-row window. First row has only 1 row in frame; second has 2. ROUND to 1 decimal (Day 3 float rule).

title: Salary vs Previous (LAG)

schema:

  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,

  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),

  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),

  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT name, salary, LAG(salary) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name) AS prev_salary, COALESCE(salary - LAG(salary) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name), 0) AS diff FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC, name
hint: LAG(salary) fetches previous row's salary in the window order. OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name) is fully deterministic — even at ties (Eve/Leo 72000). COALESCE fixes NULL for first row.

  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT name, salary, COALESCE(LEAD(salary) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name), 0) AS next_salary, COALESCE(LEAD(salary) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name) - salary, 0) AS diff FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC, name
hint: LEAD fetches the next row's value. COALESCE(..., 0) replaces NULL for the last row. Deterministic: OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name) breaks ties.

title: Days Since Previous Hire
desc: Show name, hire_date, and days between previous hire and current hire as days_since_prev, rounded to 0 decimals. First employee (Frank) shows NULL.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),

solution: SELECT name, hire_date, ROUND(julianday(hire_date) - julianday(LAG(hire_date) OVER(ORDER BY hire_date, name)), 0) AS days_since_prev FROM employees ORDER BY hire_date, name
hint: LAG(hire_date) gets the previous hire date. julianday difference gives days. First row's LAG is NULL so the result is NULL. ROUND to 0 decimals per float rule. OVER(ORDER BY hire_date, name) ensures determinism.

hint: Multiple windows can coexist in one query. Each has its own OVER clause. ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and LAG on the same data show different perspectives.

  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),

title: Centered Moving Sum (Orders)
desc: Show each order's id, product, amount, order_date, and a centered 3-row moving sum rounded to 2 decimals as centered_sum. Frame: 1 PRECEDING to 1 FOLLOWING.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL,
  order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
solution: SELECT id, product, amount, order_date, ROUND(SUM(amount) OVER(ORDER BY order_date, id ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING), 2) AS centered_sum FROM orders ORDER BY order_date, id
hint: ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING gives a 3-row centered window. The first and last rows have only 2 rows in their frame. ROUND to 2 decimals per float rule.

title: Employee Count Pivot
desc: Use COUNT with CASE to pivot department counts into columns. Show engineering, marketing, hr, and sales as columns.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);

  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),

hint: COUNT(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 END) counts only rows where the condition is true. ELSE returns nothing (NULL), which COUNT ignores.

schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE projects (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL,
  lead_id INTEGER, deadline TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL);

  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),

  (2,'Beta',3,'2024-07-15','Active'),

solution: SELECT e.department, COUNT(DISTINCT e.id) AS employees, COUNT(CASE WHEN p.id IS NOT NULL THEN 1 END) AS with_projects, COUNT(CASE WHEN p.id IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS without_projects FROM employees e LEFT JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.lead_id GROUP BY e.department ORDER BY e.department
hint: LEFT JOIN ensures employees with no projects still appear. p.id IS NULL identifies employees without projects. COUNT(DISTINCT e.id) prevents double-counting from multiple projects.

title: Hire Quarter Pivot
desc: Pivot hires into quarters using strftime and CASE. Show q1, q2, q3, q4 as columns.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1 AND 3 THEN 1 END) AS q1, COUNT(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 4 AND 6 THEN 1 END) AS q2, COUNT(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 7 AND 9 THEN 1 END) AS q3, COUNT(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 10 AND 12 THEN 1 END) AS q4 FROM employees
hint: CAST(strftime('%m', hire_date) AS INTEGER) converts the month string to a number, then BETWEEN groups months into quarters.

title: Name Pattern Pivot
desc: Count employees whose names start with 'A', end with 'e', or contain 'i' — all in separate columns using CASE and LIKE.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN name LIKE 'A%' THEN 1 END) AS starts_with_a, COUNT(CASE WHEN name LIKE '%e' THEN 1 END) AS ends_with_e, COUNT(CASE WHEN name LIKE '%i%' THEN 1 END) AS contains_i FROM employees
hint: Each COUNT(CASE WHEN ... THEN 1 END) is independent — the same row can be counted in multiple columns.

title: Running Total with Milestones
desc: Show each employee's name, hire_date, salary, a running total of salary by hire_date, and a milestone label (Below 200k, 200-400k, Above 400k).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH running AS (SELECT name, hire_date, salary, SUM(salary) OVER(ORDER BY hire_date) AS running_total FROM employees) SELECT name, hire_date, salary, running_total, CASE WHEN running_total < 200000 THEN 'Below 200k' WHEN running_total < 400000 THEN '200-400k' ELSE 'Above 400k' END AS milestone FROM running ORDER BY hire_date
hint: CTE computes the running total with SUM() OVER(). The main query adds a milestone label using CASE on the running total value.

title: Dept Stats Matrix
desc: For each department, show total employees, avg salary (rounded), total salary of high earners (>=80000), and avg salary of high earners (rounded). Order by department.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS total_employees, ROUND(AVG(salary), 0) AS avg_salary, SUM(CASE WHEN salary >= 80000 THEN salary END) AS high_earner_total, ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN salary >= 80000 THEN salary END), 0) AS high_earner_avg FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY department
hint: CASE inside AVG and SUM lets you compute statistics on subsets without a subquery. AVG(CASE ...) ignores NULLs (non-qualifying rows).

hint: The CTE computes department averages and ranks them. The main query adds a human-readable tier label with CASE.

title: High Earner Percentage by Dept
desc: For each department, show total employees, count of high earners (>=80000), and the percentage of high earners rounded to 1 decimal. Order by percentage descending.

CREATE TABLE employees (

  manager_id INTEGER);

hint: 100.0 * ... forces floating-point division. ROUND to 1 decimal. COUNT(*) in the denominator gives total per department.

title: Multi-Condition Salary Pivot
desc: Use SUM with CASE and ELSE to count employees in salary tiers: top_tier (>=90000), senior (80000-89999), mid (60000-79999), junior (<60000).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN salary >= 90000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS top_tier, SUM(CASE WHEN salary >= 80000 AND salary < 90000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS senior, SUM(CASE WHEN salary >= 60000 AND salary < 80000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS mid, SUM(CASE WHEN salary < 60000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS junior FROM employees
hint: SUM(CASE ... THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) works like COUNT but with explicit ELSE 0. This is the standard SQL pivot pattern. Note the non-overlapping ranges.

title: Salary Gap Analysis (LEAD + CASE)
desc: Use a CTE with ROW_NUMBER and LEAD, then label the gap between consecutive salaries as 'Big Gap' (>10k), 'Medium Gap' (>5k), or 'Small Gap'. Order by rank.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH ranked AS (SELECT name, salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn, LEAD(salary) OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name) AS next_salary FROM employees) SELECT name, salary, rn, COALESCE(next_salary, 0) AS next_salary, CASE WHEN next_salary IS NULL THEN 'Lowest' WHEN salary - next_salary > 10000 THEN 'Big Gap' WHEN salary - next_salary > 5000 THEN 'Medium Gap' ELSE 'Small Gap' END AS gap_type FROM ranked ORDER BY rn
hint: LEAD(salary) gets the next row's salary. The difference tells you how far apart consecutive salaries are. COALESCE handles the last row. Deterministic: OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC, name).

title: Ultimate Department Report
desc: Using a CTE, compute per-department stats (count, avg salary, max salary, high earner count, percentage high). Then add RANK and a composition label. Order by salary rank.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH dept_data AS (SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count, ROUND(AVG(salary), 0) AS avg_salary, MAX(salary) AS max_salary, SUM(CASE WHEN salary >= 80000 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS high_count FROM employees GROUP BY department) SELECT department, emp_count, avg_salary, max_salary, high_count, ROUND(100.0 * high_count / emp_count, 1) AS pct_high, RANK() OVER(ORDER BY avg_salary DESC) AS salary_rank, CASE WHEN high_count = emp_count THEN 'All High' WHEN high_count > emp_count / 2 THEN 'Mostly High' ELSE 'Mixed' END AS composition FROM dept_data ORDER BY salary_rank
hint: This is a 'kitchen sink' query: CTE for aggregation, multiple columns with ROUND, RANK OVER the CTE results, CASE for composition. Each concept solves one part of the report.

title: Org Chart with Role Type
desc: Use a recursive CTE for the org chart, then label each employee's role: 'Executive' at level 0, 'Engineer' for Engineering at level >0, 'Staff' otherwise. Order by level then name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (SELECT id, name, department, manager_id, 0 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.name, e.department, e.manager_id, ot.level + 1 FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id) SELECT id, name, department, level, CASE WHEN level = 0 THEN 'Executive' WHEN department = 'Engineering' AND level > 0 THEN 'Engineer' ELSE 'Staff' END AS role_type FROM org_tree ORDER BY level, name
hint: Recursive CTE builds the tree, then CASE labels based on position in the hierarchy. The ORDER BY puts executives first, then alphabetically.
