  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH tenure AS (SELECT name, hire_date, ROUND((julianday('2025-01-01') - julianday(hire_date)) / 365.25, 1) AS years_tenure FROM employees) SELECT name, hire_date, years_tenure, NTILE(4) OVER(ORDER BY years_tenure DESC) AS tenure_quartile, CASE WHEN years_tenure >= 5 THEN 'Veteran' WHEN years_tenure >= 3 THEN 'Established' ELSE 'New' END AS tenure_band FROM tenure ORDER BY years_tenure DESC
hint: julianday difference / 365.25 gives annual tenure. NTILE(4) divides employees into 4 quartiles. CASE buckets into Veteran/Established/New. Fixed reference date, no 'now'.

title: Employee Spending Analysis
desc: For each employee, count big orders (>=1000), small orders (<1000), and total amount spent. Include employees with no orders. Order by total spent descending then name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, employee_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  product TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL,
  order_date TEXT NOT NULL);
solution: SELECT e.name, e.department, COUNT(CASE WHEN o.amount >= 1000 THEN 1 END) AS big_orders, COUNT(CASE WHEN o.amount < 1000 AND o.amount > 0 THEN 1 END) AS small_orders, ROUND(COALESCE(SUM(o.amount), 0), 2) AS total_spent FROM employees e LEFT JOIN orders o ON e.id = o.employee_id GROUP BY e.id ORDER BY total_spent DESC, e.name
hint: LEFT JOIN to include all employees. CASE inside COUNT for order size buckets. COALESCE turns NULL sum (no orders) into 0. ROUND to 2 decimals for REAL amounts.

title: The Grand Report
desc: Combine EVERYTHING: recursive CTE for org chart, CTE for dept averages, window function for dept salary rank, and CASE for above/below dept avg. Order by level, dept avg descending, then salary descending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (SELECT id, name, department, salary, manager_id, 0 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.name, e.department, e.salary, e.manager_id, ot.level + 1 FROM employees e INNER JOIN org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id), dept_stats AS (SELECT department, ROUND(AVG(salary), 0) AS dept_avg FROM employees GROUP BY department) SELECT o.id, o.name, o.department, o.salary, o.level, RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY o.department ORDER BY o.salary DESC) AS dept_salary_rank, d.dept_avg, CASE WHEN o.salary > d.dept_avg THEN 'Above Avg' WHEN o.salary = d.dept_avg THEN 'At Avg' ELSE 'Below Avg' END AS vs_dept_avg FROM org_tree o INNER JOIN dept_stats d ON o.department = d.department ORDER BY o.level, d.dept_avg DESC, o.salary DESC
hint: This combines recursive CTEs (org hierarchy), non-recursive CTEs (dept stats), window functions (rank within department), and CASE (above/below average). Each concept contributes one piece of the final report.

title: Name Length Pivot
desc: Use LENGTH and CASE to pivot employees by name length: six_plus_chars (length >= 6), five_chars (length = 5), four_or_less (length <= 4).
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department TEXT NOT NULL,
  salary INTEGER NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, city TEXT NOT NULL,
  manager_id INTEGER);
data:
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice','Engineering',85000,'2020-01-15','New York',6),
  (2,'Bob','Marketing',65000,'2019-03-22','Chicago',5),
  (3,'Charlie','Engineering',92000,'2018-07-01','New York',6),
  (4,'Diana','HR',58000,'2021-06-10','Boston',7),
  (5,'Eve','Marketing',72000,'2020-11-30','Chicago',NULL),
  (6,'Frank','Engineering',95000,'2017-02-14','San Francisco',NULL),
  (7,'Grace','HR',61000,'2019-09-05','Boston',NULL),
  (8,'Hank','Sales',55000,'2022-01-20','New York',9),
  (9,'Ivy','Sales',67000,'2020-04-18','Chicago',NULL),
  (10,'Jack','Engineering',88000,'2021-08-25','San Francisco',6),
  (11,'Kate','Engineering',78000,'2022-06-01','New York',1),
  (12,'Leo','Engineering',72000,'2023-01-10','Boston',1),
  (13,'Mia','Marketing',54000,'2023-03-15','Chicago',5),
  (14,'Noah','Engineering',81000,'2022-09-01','San Francisco',10);
solution: SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN LENGTH(name) >= 6 THEN 1 END) AS six_plus_chars, COUNT(CASE WHEN LENGTH(name) = 5 THEN 1 END) AS five_chars, COUNT(CASE WHEN LENGTH(name) <= 4 THEN 1 END) AS four_or_less FROM employees
hint: LENGTH(name) counts characters. The pivot groups into 3 buckets by character count. Each employee falls into exactly one bucket because the ranges are non-overlapping.
